title: Requirements Manager (f/d/m)
required_skills: Engineering/technical degree or equivalent practical experience
Experience in engineering projects across different project phases
Knowledge of requirements management methodologies and tools
matched_skills: Cross-functional coordination across engineering, product and business teams (HSBC, Hapag-Lloyd)
Documentation and structured reporting (HSBC programme documentation, Hapag-Lloyd sprint reports)
Stakeholder alignment and communication across project phases (HSBC 6 workstreams)
Risk and dependency management (HSBC: risk escalation and prioritisation)
missing_skills: Requirements management methodology and tool knowledge (e.g. DOORS, JAMA)
Railway or engineering project domain experience
Requirements traceability and change impact assessment skills
core_responsibilities: Define and drive requirements management strategy across all project phases
Ensure consistency, quality and traceability of requirements from customer specs to delivery
Coordinate stakeholders in drafting, aligning and validating requirements
Manage requirement changes and assess impact on scope, timelines and costs
Monitor progress, risks and deviations to support project decision-making
Contribute to continuous improvement of RM tools, processes and best practices
recommended_emphasis: HSBC: Central coordination interface across 6 workstreams — demonstrates requirements-like scope and dependency management
Hapag-Lloyd: Sprint planning and coordination between product owners and teams — relevant to requirement intake and validation flow
ECE Group: Structured analysis and progress reporting — transferable to requirements documentation discipline
JIRA proficiency — relevant to requirements tracking workflows
summary: Boru scores 36.4 (weak match) for this technically-oriented requirements management role in railway engineering. The core gap is the engineering/technical background and specialised requirements management methodology knowledge that the role presupposes. Boru's cross-functional coordination and documentation strengths partially align with the softer aspects of the role but do not bridge the technical requirements engineering dimension. Application is possible as a stretch but should acknowledge the domain gap directly.
